text: The surgeon lifts the stomach and performs a partial omentectomy along the greater curvature, extending towards the tip of the spleen. The left gastroepiploic vessels are isolated, ligated, and transected.

The surgeon cleans the stomach along the greater curvature to access lymph node number four, D, while lifting the stomach. On the right side of the patient, the partial omentectomy continues with assistance traction pulling the mesocolon downward and outward to aid dissection along the fusion embryology plane between the mesocolon and the greater omentum.

The assistant pulls down the pancreas to facilitate dissection along the common hepatic artery. The operator carefully manipulates the tissue to avoid injury to the pyloric branch of the hepatic artery. Dissection continues along the suprapancreatic border towards the splenic artery, following the completion of infrapyloric area dissection.

The surgeon continues dissection along the left gastric artery, exposing the coronary vein. The coronary vein is ligated and transected. The left gastric artery is lifted, double clipped, and transected.

The surgeon returns to the infrapyloric area, where the bluish mesocolic vessels are visible. The partial omentectomy continues, with careful dissection to avoid injury to the mesocolic vessels or transverse colon, particularly near the hepatic flexure. The dissection progresses toward the second part of the duodenum.

The surgeon exposes the second part of the duodenum, with the assistant applying downward traction on the mesocolon. Gentle handling of the mesocolon and omentum allows for division of the fusion area. Dissection continues along the mesocolon vessel toward the pancreas, using traction and counter-traction to safely identify the fusion plane.

The surgeon continues the omentectomy, dissecting toward the infraborder of the pancreas. The dissection progresses along the right gastroepiploic vein and artery, with careful preservation and separation of the infrapyretic vessels.

The surgeon dissects the medial side of the right gastroepiploic vessels, ensuring no injury to the infrapyloric vessels. The infrapyloric vessel is visible below. After bifurcation, the right gastroepiploic vessels are ligated and transected. The greater curvature of the antrum is cleared approximately five centimeters. The operator places three gauzes to expand the lesser omentum, facilitating identification and dissection along the lesser curvature.

The surgeon begins dissection approximately three centimeters from the pylorus, exposing the gastric arcade on the posterior side of the lesser omentum. The gastric arcade is ligated and transected. The hepatic branch of the vagus nerve is visualized, and lymph nodes along area number one are carefully dissected away from the hepatic branch using an inactive blade of an energy device to prevent injury to underlying tissues.

The surgeon continues dissection along the cardia area, focusing on the posterior side. The lymphovascular tissue on the posterior side of the cardia and upper stomach is cleared, moving to the anterior side. The lymphovasculature along the cardia, including the left scapula, is cleared. The coronary vein and left gastric artery in the anterior to posterior side of the stomach are cleared, preparing for anastomosis. Using a vertical mini laparotomy, the stomach is extracted and transected three centimeters from the pylorus.

The surgeon sends a portion of the stomach for frozen section confirmation. The tumor is localized, and proximal resection is performed on the greater curvature using an Allen clamp. The lesser curvature is created with long linear staples. The proximal stomach is prepared for anastomosis to the remnant distal antrum, which is achieved using one layer of continuous interlocking sutures.
